speaker: interviewer
text: Today is March 27, 1995. This is an interview with Barbara Traub. I'm the interviewer, Amy Friedman. We are in New York in the United States of America conducting an interview in English. It's March 27, 1995. This is an interview with Barbara Traub. I am Amy Friedman. We are in New York in the United States of America, and we are speaking in English.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, the--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, Mrs. Traub, why don't we start with where you were born and your maiden name and when you were born.

speaker: interviewee
text: My maiden name is Fischman. First name was Golda, but my nickname and a name I was-- still am mostly known by is Baba. I was born on September 7, 1925 in a town called Sighet, or Sziget. At the time, it was Romania, and this town is in northern Transylvania.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell Sighet for us just for the--

speaker: interviewee
text: The Romanian spelling is S-I-G-H-E-T. And eventually, later on when the Hungarians occupied that part of Romania, it became Sziget. With a different spelling, S-Z-I-G-E-T.

speaker: interviewer
text: So can you tell us about life in Sighet for you as a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. As I said, I was born after the First World War in 1925. Prior to that, Sighet and Transylvania was part of the Austro-Hungarian empire, and the reason I mention this is that I was bilingual. My parents were a part of the Austrian-- Austro-Hungarian regime and life. And the language spoken at home was Hungarian. My memories of my childhood are the best of my life because I had a very loving, caring, wonderful father and mother, two brothers, and a sister who pampered me very much. I was the youngest of the four children. And I had aunts and uncles, and it was a very warm, supportive, protective family and a well-to-do family. And we had a very full, rich life. From early on, there were two things guiding my life, and that was to get an education, to learn, learning, to be Jewish, and in those days, to be a nice, in quotes, girl, which meant to be very soft, modest, as my mother used to say, and just be nice. And so this is how I grew up. I don't think I was always very submissive, but by and large, I went along, and it was an easy life, a good life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us about each person in your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I was the youngest of four children. The oldest child in my family was my sister. Her name was Janka in Hungarian, or Jenny, Jennifer, she would probably be called here. She was a very, very beautiful girl, and the-- at the age of 18, she got married. And six months later, she died. And that was the greatest tragedy in my mother's life short of, of course, what happened later. Because she died at the not quite 19, and it was a beautiful marriage, a lovely marriage. I remember it clearly. I was about five years old. And she contracted double pneumonia. In those days, they had no penicillin and no help to combat this. And she died in two days. And it was a very great tragedy that affected my mother, as I said, till the last moments of her life. She never stopped wearing black. [SIGHS] And after that came a son by the name of Mano, or Moshe. He was born, I believe, around 1915 or so. Then came brother Alex, or Sandor, as he was called. He was born in 1917, and I was born in 1925, so there was a great span in years.

speaker: interviewee
text: And as I learned from my parents, I wasn't really planned. I happened. And so when my sister died, I remember my aunt, who was very, very close to us. I remember hearing her say to my mother, you see, Regina? Aren't you glad that you at least have another little girl? And that little girl was I.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a great loss, and she was a beautiful, beautiful girl, young woman. But the family-- I also remember my grandmother, my mother's mother, grandmother Miriam. What was extraordinary about her was that she was a schoolteacher. Now to be-- in her younger years before she married my grandfather, whom I never knew. But in those days-- I'm speaking about early 20th century or late 19th century. First of all, for a woman to be a schoolteacher was extraordinary. And for a Jewish woman to be a schoolteacher, it was doubly extraordinary. But she was, and I remember my grandmother Miriam teaching me the names of flowers as a little girl, teaching me how to count. I, I, I remember her with such warmth and such reverence, and she died.

speaker: interviewer
text: You say that it was extraordinary for a woman and a Jew. Can you talk more about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, because although, as I understand and from what I've read and learned, there was no overt anti-Semitism in Austro-Hungary. It was sub rosa. It was always underneath. And so certain things, Jews did not do because, first of all, they knew ahead of time that they could not succeed. And secondly, particularly in my grandmother's case, I would imagine that for a Jewish family in those days-- and they were very religious, as I understood, orthodox-- to allow their daughter to go out and teach was in and of itself something very special.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in a way, that was almost translated many, many years later when I told my parents as a teenager, 14 or 15, that I want to go and study law. And that was, you know, doing something against the accepted norm. A girl, at best, will have her baccalaureate. Even that was already quite an achievement. And after that, she's getting married. Meanwhile, she would learn to play the piano, speak a little French. But to go to law school, who ever heard of that?

speaker: interviewee
text: But I did feel that I, I did-- I would have liked to. And in fact, my older brother, Mano, who was a wonderful influence on my life and who was regarded with a great deal of respect because-- by my parents and so he sort of assured me, don't worry, Baba. When the time comes, I'll interfere and intervene, and you will go to your law school. Well, things didn't turn out quite that way.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your grandmother a good-- you said that the most important thing was to be a good girl. Was she considered a good girl?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] She must have been because she was a wonderful mother and grandmother. And I remembered that my mother and her brother would more or less fight over her with whom she should stay. This is how much beloved she was. So she would stay six months with us-- that's-- these are my memories of her-- and six months in my uncle's house. And in those six months, my mother and father would visit her practically every evening. So she must have been a good person.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was Jewish life like in Sighet?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a very active Jewish life in my days. When I say in my days, I'm, I'm beginning with the Romanian occupation-- well, I shouldn't say occupation-- but the Romanian regime, because the Trianon Treaty during the First World War awarded the entire Transylvania to Romania. And while my parents were born there, and my grandparents and so was I, we were switching governments. I mean, I was born under Romanian government, but ultimately, it was the Hungarian government who did what it did to us. Through the Nazis, I should add. But going back to the Jewish life, it was a good, strong Jewish life. In fact, I remember it was idealistic in the sense that the youth was idealistic. We belonged-- most of my friends, including me-- belonged to Zionist groups. I belonged to the junior Zionist group called Habonim. And the older girls-- older girls, two, three year older, older than I-- belonged to the Barisia, which was a step forward. And but Jewish life was good in the sense that they-- the Jews very permitted-- there was no obstacle to be Jewish. Business was closed during Saturday. The main street, the corso, was full of-- around 12:00, noon time-- it was full of Jews coming from synagogues, after synagogue, having a walk on the corso. Business stopped during Saturdays. I'm talking about the Romanian regime.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just to give a context, I forgot to say, how big was Sighet, and which part of that was Jewish or not?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. Sighet was-- [LAUGHS] today we would consider it a little village, but it was not really a village. It had about 50,000 people. There were Jews, Hungarians, and Romanians. Jews were about between 12,000 and 15,000 Jews, and the rest was a mix-- mixture of ethnic Hungarians and Romanians. During the Romanian regime, I do not remember any anti-Semitism. Things went along nicely. My father was a well-known and respected businessman. They-- he was in textile, and his specialty were silks, as another businessman's specialty were woolens. So my father's specialty was silk. And it was a very comfortable, to say the least, household in which I grew up.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it like between the Jews and the Hungarians? What was the relationship in the town?

speaker: interviewee
text: During the Romanian regime, there was no relationship because, first of all, they were minorities. And this would eventually change in 1940 when the Hungarians took over. But the relationship with the Romanians was a very good relationship in the sense that it was a practical relationship. There was a Jewish community. I don't remember the name what it was called. Well, it was the Jewish community with a president, with a-- the [NON-ENGLISH]. My uncle was at one time the [NON-ENGLISH], and my uncle was a very stern man. And the story is-- as a matter of fact, I do remember what happened. There was a great deal of goings-on between the town across the river-- because Sighet was between two rivers, Tisza and Isza. Well, across Tisza, it was Czechoslovakia, and there was a lot of contraband going on between Czechoslovakia and our town. The contraband consisted, I think, well, the Czechs at that time had very special-- were, I believe, woollens. And the English woollens were much less expensive if you used-- you know, brought it in from Czechoslovakia. At any rate, the officers, the duty officers were bribed. So they turned an eye. They turned away and permitted the contraband to continue, except-- and this happened during my uncle's presidency as being the [NON-ENGLISH]-- somebody was caught very badly. And in order to extricate him from jail or god knows what other terrible things-- which included really nothing more than jail for a couple of days-- the [NON-ENGLISH]-- I forgot now the word for [NON-ENGLISH] in English, not immigration authorities. The, the custom authorities wanted an enormous amount of money from the Jewish community not to-- not to bring him to justice, this man who was caught with the contraband. Well, my uncle, being a stern man and having principles, he said the following. I am going to have the fine paid, but henceforth, no more contraband between Czechoslovakia and Sighet because it's illegal, and it's not right, and it's finished. No more contraband. Well, he was not reelected next year because while there was no contraband, business wasn't that good anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in other words, what I'm trying to say, there was a sort of arrangement between the Romanians and the Jews, but more important-- I mean, this is from a business point of view-- but more importantly, there was no interference about going to synagogues. Everybody did his or her thing.

speaker: interviewee
text: In fact, I remember one of my friends as a child, as a-- when I was in elementary school, was the son-- his name was Petra Shurt, the son of the commander-- the commandant of the Romanian garrison in Sighet. He was a friend of mine. I have pictures of a birthday with this Petra Shur and some other friends of mine. I never experienced anti-Semitism during the Romanian regime.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about school.

speaker: interviewee
text: I started, of course, kindergarten, and then there was a special school in Sighet where they were-- an elementary school, part of the teaching institute where they were training teachers. And the elementary school, I think it had four, four grades. And it just received or it accepted a certain number, clearly, of children, and I was enrolled. And I still to this day I remember the name of some of my teachers, a Mr. Radu a Mr. Purvan, who were young men. Eventually, they became accredited teachers. And it was, again, a wonderful school. We played with each other, Jews and non-Jews alike. And after that came the gymnasium or high school or liceu [ROMANIAN]. That was the name of the Romanian high school. There too, it was quite accepted. I mean, I was as much part of the whole milieu as anybody else, if not more so because I participated in all kinds of things.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like what?

speaker: interviewee
text: And what brought this life to an end was the beginning of the Second World War, although there were already clearly news and rumors. Not so much rumors, but news about someone called Hitler, whom my father used to say, a madman. I mean, normal people don't listen to madmen. Go ahead.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did those rumors start seeping in, and how did they start seeping in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in the early '30s, I remember-- because I was about six, seven years old-- I remember the grownups, you know, during dinner parties or when people came to visit my parents, talking about what was happening in Germany, that the government fell, and then the Reichstag was-- the burning of the Reichstag. And that there was this terrible man who had these peculiar ideas, but again, I heard the grownups say, a nation that produced Bach, Beethoven, and Brahms-- how can they believe this madman? It shall pass.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that began in the '30s. And it was again under Romanian regime, and life was good. Let's go back to-- you were telling us about Shabbat. Can you bring us through?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. My mother's household was kosher. Dairies were separate from the meat dishes. The cook was-- we always had Jewish cooks because they would know how to handle that. And they were generally very nice young women who came from little villages around town and who wanted to probably get some dowry together, and that's why they came to families who could afford them. And they stayed for a couple of years until they amassed enough to hopefully find a man and have the dowry. We were religious. My father did not wear a yamaka. My mother did not have a wig, but they observed all the holidays. Shabbat was Shabbat. Friday night, my father went to shul with the boys, with my brothers. Women did not go to shul on Friday night, at least not in Sighet, and not, not in Eastern Europe, I would assume. Once, I remember being very reprimanded because it was Friday, and I was involved in some project in school. And lo and behold, I forgot time. And as-- and-- and I-- as I came in-- and I remember my parents' home very well-- on the foyer, I could see the dining room, and I saw they were already sitting at the table. And I knew I was late for the lighting of the Shabbat candles. And indeed, I was very badly reprimanded and made to understand that school can wait. When Friday night comes and Shabbat sets in, I better be home. And I think I've never missed again. The holidays, the Jewish holidays and holy days were observed all the way. It came Pesach, or Erev Pesach, I remember my father going from corner to corner and made sure that Chometz was not there. I remember came Sukkot. I remember making those beautiful birds with my father to decorate the sukkah, and he had a way of, of emptying the eggs so that he would just have the eggshell and then putting in some colored paper for the bird's tail and so forth. It was wonderful to do these things with my father. And the Jewishness was very important, in fact, to the point that I believe I was one of the very few girls who had a Talmudic student come to the house regularly once a week to teach me how to read Hebrew and pray, to read the siddur. At the same time, it was a secular household because I went to a secular school. The language we spoke was Hungarian. My brothers went to secular schools. They were, of course, bar mitzvahed. And I have vague memories of seeing my brothers for many, many years putting on the tefillin in the mornings. It was a traditional home. What was it like to learn Talmud when no one else was learning it? Well, [LAUGHS] I guess I did resent it because it was taking me away from other things. But I accepted it because I understood that this was part of our heritage, and I have to do it. And besides, I saw my mother reading the-- came Saturday afternoons, she would leaf through-- there was a library with those huge, very impressive books, which I learned later they were Midrashi texts and then Talmudi texts. And I mean, I just-- yes, I wasn't too happy. I would have much rather gone out and played tennis or bicycle or whatever I would have done, but I did it anyway. I did it. And life went on. Life went on, and it was a good life. It was a good life.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were telling us about high school before everything changed. Can you go back there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It was-- my first four years in high school were under the Romanian regime. And I was very active in school. I ran a paper-- or, or I shouldn't say I ran a paper, but I was the editor of the school paper. And then we put up some plays, and then I was involved in that. I loved studying. I did. And my parents supported me very, very much. I mean, they never had to make sure that I did my homework. I just liked very much, but I was lucky. I had so many good teachers. Even then, I remembered one of my teachers, literature. She was a Romanian lady, Dwana Vacielescu. And I remember my French teacher, Mademoiselle Cordeoneau. And it was a full life, a healthy life, a stimulating life, even for a young girl as I was at the time about 12 or so. And it was a happy life.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to stop the tape.
